g.data-narration: The Waterfall Model - A sequential software development methodology developed by Winston Royce in 1970.
The Waterfall Model is a systematic, sequential approach where each phase must complete before the next begins.
g.text: A linear, sequential software development methodology where each phase
must be completed before the next begins. Progress flows steadily
downward through phases like a waterfall. Developed by Winston W.
Royce in 1970. Suitable for projects with stable requirements.

g.data-narration: The model consists of five sequential phases: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, and Maintenance.
Phase 1 gathers and documents all requirements. The output is a comprehensive SRS document serving as the blueprint.
Phase 2 creates system architecture based on SRS. Output includes data flow diagrams, database schema, and interface specs.
Phase 3 writes actual code. Each module is tested independently through unit testing to verify correct functionality.
Phase 4 integrates modules and performs integration and system testing to verify the complete system against requirements.
Phase 5 deploys the system to production. Maintenance fixes defects and implements enhancements over the software lifecycle.
Key characteristics include sequential execution, extensive documentation, formal reviews, and a top-down approach.
Advantages include simplicity, well-defined deliverables, suitability for stable requirements, clear milestones, and early quality assurance.
g.text: Simplicity & Clarity - Easy to understand and implement
Well-Defined Deliverables - Each phase produces specific tangible outputs
Stable Requirements - Highly effective when requirements are fixed
Clear Milestones - Easy progress tracking & management

g.data-narration: Disadvantages include inflexibility to changes, late testing, long delivery time, high risk, and poor handling of uncertainty.
g.text: Inflexibility - Changes to completed phases are extremely expensive
Late Testing - Fundamental design flaws discovered too late
Long Delivery - Working software only at project end
High Risk - Building wrong product until final phase
